If you’re preparing for a JavaScript interview, these are some of the most commonly asked questions. In this article, I’ll explain each one in simple language with examples so that you can confidently answer them in your next interview.

1. What is the difference between let, var, and const?
These are all used to declare variables in JavaScript, but they behave differently.
Feature: var let const
Scope: Function Block Block
Can Reassign?: ✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ No
Can Redeclare?:✅ Yes❌ No❌ No
Hoisted: Yes Yes (TDZ) Yes (TDZ)
var

• Function scoped

• Can be redeclared

• Can be reassigned
var name = "John";
var name = "Mike"; // Allowedconsole.log(name); // Mikelet

• Block scoped

• Cannot be redeclared

• Can be reassigned
let age = 20;
age = 25;console.log(age); // 25const

• Block scoped

• Cannot be redeclared

• Cannot be reassigned
const PI = 3.14;// PI = 5; ❌ ErrorInterview Answer
var is function-scoped and can be redeclared and reassigned. let is block-scoped, cannot be redeclared but can be reassigned. const is also block-scoped, but it cannot be reassigned after declaration.

2. What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)?
The Temporal Dead Zone is the period between entering a scope and declaring a variable using let or const.
During this time, the variable exists but cannot be accessed.
console.log(name);let name = "Sumit";Output
ReferenceErrorWhy?
Because let variables are hoisted but are not initialized immediately.
The same happens with const.
console.log(age);const age = 20;Interview Answer
The Temporal Dead Zone is the time between entering a block and declaring a let or const variable. Accessing the variable during this period throws a ReferenceError.

3. Difference between Spread and Rest Operator
Both use ..., but their purpose is different.
Spread Operator
It expands elements.
const arr1 = [1,2];
const arr2 = [...arr1,3,4];console.log(arr2);Output
[1,2,3,4]Another example:
const user = {
name: "John"
};const newUser = {
...user,
age: 25
};Rest Operator
It collects multiple values into one.
function add(...numbers){
console.log(numbers);
}add(1,2,3,4);Output
[1,2,3,4]Interview Answer
Spread operator expands arrays or objects, whereas Rest operator collects multiple values into a single array.

4. Difference between Arrow Function and Normal Function
Normal Function
function greet(){
console.log("Hello");
}Arrow Function
const greet = () => {
console.log("Hello");
};Main Differences
1. this
Normal function has its own this.
Arrow function inherits this from its parent.
Example:
const person = {
name: "John", normal(){
console.log(this.name);
}, arrow: () => {
console.log(this.name);
}
};person.normal(); // John
person.arrow(); // undefined2. Constructor
Normal functions can be used as constructors.
function Person(){}
const p = new Person();Arrow functions cannot.
const Person = () => {};// new Person(); ❌ Error3. Arguments Object
Normal functions have an arguments object.
Arrow functions do not.
Interview Answer
Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax and inherit this from the surrounding scope. Normal functions have their own this, can be used as constructors, and have an arguments object.

5. Difference Between Deep Copy and Shallow Copy
Shallow Copy
Only the first level is copied.
Nested objects still share the same reference.
const user = {
name: "John",
address: {
city: "Delhi"
}
};const copy = {...user};copy.address.city = "Mumbai";console.log(user.address.city);Output
MumbaiThe original object changed.
Deep Copy
Everything is copied independently.
const user = {
name: "John",
address: {
city: "Delhi"
}
};const copy = structuredClone(user);copy.address.city = "Mumbai";console.log(user.address.city);Output
DelhiInterview Answer
A shallow copy copies only the first level, while nested objects still share references. A deep copy creates completely independent copies of nested objects.

6. What are Promises?
A Promise is an object that represents the future result of an asynchronous operation.
A Promise has three states.

• Pending

• Fulfilled

• Rejected
Example
const promise = new Promise((resolve,reject)=>{ let success = true; if(success){
resolve("Success");
}else{
reject("Failed");
}});promise
.then(res=>console.log(res))
.catch(err=>console.log(err));Why do we use Promises?
Before promises, developers used nested callbacks.
This created Callback Hell.
Promises make asynchronous code cleaner and easier to manage.
Interview Answer
A Promise represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It helps write cleaner asynchronous code and avoids callback hell.

7. Promises vs Async/Await
Both are used for asynchronous programming.
Promise
fetch(url)
.then(res=>res.json())
.then(data=>console.log(data))
.catch(err=>console.log(err));Async/Await
async function getData(){ try{ const response = await fetch(url);
const data = await response.json(); console.log(data); }catch(error){ console.log(error); }}Which one do you prefer?
Most developers prefer Async/Await because

• Easier to read

• Looks like synchronous code

• Better error handling using try/catch
Interview Answer
I prefer async/await because it makes asynchronous code cleaner, easier to understand, and simplifies error handling using try/catch. Internally, async/await is built on top of promises.

8. What are Closures?
A closure is created when an inner function remembers variables from its outer function even after the outer function has finished executing.
Example
function outer(){ let count = 0; return function(){ count++; console.log(count); }}const counter = outer();counter();
counter();
counter();Output
1
2
3Even though outer() has already finished, the inner function still remembers count.
Real-world Uses

• Data privacy

• Counters

• Memoization

• Event handlers
Interview Answer
A closure is a function that remembers variables from its outer scope even after the outer function has finished executing.

9. What are Generator Functions?
Generator functions allow us to pause and resume execution.
They are declared using function*.
Example
function* numbers(){ yield 1;
yield 2;
yield 3;}const gen = numbers();console.log(gen.next());
console.log(gen.next());
console.log(gen.next());Output
{value:1, done:false}
{value:2, done:false}
{value:3, done:false}Uses

• Lazy loading

• Large datasets

• Custom iterators
Interview Answer
Generator functions can pause and resume execution using the yield keyword. They are useful for handling sequences of values efficiently.

10. Difference Between call(), apply(), and bind()
These methods are used to control the value of this.
Example
const person = { name:"Sumit"};function greet(city){ console.log(this.name, city);}call()
Arguments are passed individually.
greet.call(person,"Mumbai");apply()
Arguments are passed as an array.
greet.apply(person,["Mumbai"]);bind()
Returns a new function.
const newFunction = greet.bind(person);newFunction("Mumbai");Interview Answer
call() invokes a function immediately with arguments passed individually. apply() also invokes immediately but takes arguments as an array. bind() does not execute immediately; instead, it returns a new function with a fixed this value.

11. Coding Question
Count the frequency of each character in a string.
Input
helloOutput
{
h:1,
e:1,
l:2,
o:1
}Solution
function countCharacters(str){ const result = {}; for(const char of str){ result[char] = (result[char] || 0) + 1; } return result;}console.log(countCharacters("hello"));Time Complexity
O(n)Space Complexity
O(k)k = Number of unique charactersInterview Explanation
We create an empty object and iterate through each character of the string. If the character already exists, we increment its count; otherwise, we initialize it with 1. Finally, we return the object containing the frequency of each character.

Final Thoughts
These are some of the most commonly asked JavaScript interview questions for frontend and full-stack developer roles. Instead of memorizing answers, focus on understanding the concepts and practicing the examples. Once you understand the “why” behind each topic, answering interview questions becomes much easier.
In the next part, we’ll cover important Node.js interview questions, including the Event Loop, Streams, Middleware, Authentication with JWT, Worker Threads, WebSockets, Performance Optimization, and more.
